scenario: A telco operator team plans a migration that converts a parallel multi-instance body into a sequential multi-instance body for the same activity, expecting around 50 orders to switch execution order. The architect needs to confirm whether this kind of change between parallel and sequential multi-instance markers is permitted by the migration tool in Camunda 8.
question: Which statement correctly describes the supported direction of multi-instance body migration between parallel and sequential markers?

options.text: It is not possible to migrate a parallel multi-instance body to a sequential multi-instance body, nor to migrate a sequential multi-instance body to a parallel one
A parallel multi-instance body can be migrated to a sequential multi-instance body only if the loop cardinality is fixed at deployment time and matches between source and target
A sequential multi-instance body can be migrated to a parallel multi-instance body only when fewer than 10 child instances have already started in the current execution window
A parallel multi-instance body can be migrated to a sequential multi-instance body when the input collection variable is migrated separately as a global variable before the migration command runs

a.text: Correct. It is not possible to migrate a parallel multi-instance body to a sequential multi-instance body, and the reverse direction is equally not allowed. The multi-instance marker type must remain the same across source and target for a migration plan to be valid.

b.text: Incorrect. Loop cardinality has no influence on this restriction. The multi-instance marker conversion is not allowed at all, regardless of whether the loop cardinality is fixed or dynamic in the source and target.

c.text: Incorrect. The number of already-started child instances is not the gating factor. Migration between sequential and parallel multi-instance markers is unsupported in both directions, independent of how many child instances exist.

d.text: Incorrect. Pre-migrating the input collection variable does not unlock multi-instance marker conversion. The marker type itself cannot change during a migration, regardless of how variables are prepared beforehand.

explanation: Multi-instance body migration has a strict limitation: the marker type cannot change. A parallel multi-instance body cannot be migrated to a sequential one and a sequential multi-instance body cannot be migrated to a parallel one.
